key_points: Housing shortage forced students into converted office buildings last fall
Laundry is now free — recent positive change for dormers
Groceries can't be delivered to dorms; nearest store is a walk to Stop & Shop
key_quotes: CCSU will have a shortage for a while — they had to reopen an office building as a reshall last fall
Nothing can be delivered directly to dorms, your best bet is to go to Stop and Shop
Oh heck yes! I'm going to be full-time dorming this semester, so [free laundry] is great

key_points: Party scene exists Wed–Sat, Phi Delt reportedly draws up to 500 people
Commuters strongly advised to join clubs early — social life requires effort
Dorm social life described as chaotic, including 'backstabbing and obnoxious' residents
key_quotes: It isn't bad. There's plenty of parties usually Wednesday thru Saturday. Up to 500 at phi delt sometimes
It was fucking chaos... I've never met so many trashy, backstabbing, weird, obnoxious assholes anywhere else in my life
There are clubs available to accept new incoming students... primary place where most things take place is the Corq app

key_points: Dining hall (Memorial) is widely disliked — multiple students say avoid it
Devil's Den is the preferred option; Qdoba reportedly opening soon
Weekend dining quality drops noticeably compared to weekdays
key_quotes: Please do not eat at mem — either pack a lunch, or choose a meal plan with a lot of flex bucks
I went to the dining hall once and haven't gone back. It's just not worth the cost
It's solid enough Mon-Fri, Saturday and Sunday it's pretty meh though

key_points: Very little direct mention of counseling or mental health services in student posts
Students describe extreme academic stress, especially in engineering courses
One Reddit post from a Connecticut student references a prior mental health crisis and dropping out of a state school
key_quotes: It's like when I'm wrong I'm wrong, and when I'm right I'm still wrong... it shouldn't be impossible problem sets with 50-70 questions
That's why I left CCSU
I think they do offer some services as part of the 'blanket of support'

key_points: Scholarships are available but release dates are vague — expect late June or July
Part-time students risk losing full scholarship amounts; aid scales with enrollment
Financial aid rules are changing frequently — students advised to contact office directly
key_quotes: Financial aid has been changing every year especially with the current administration
Because of weird stuff, I had to do part time and with that the scholarship became half of the full time scholarship
Central offers a lot of [aid options] — I suggest taking that question in person to financial aid

key_points: Professor quality is highly variable — specific professors make or break courses
Math department has a long-standing reputation as weak and disorganized
STEM programs are functional but clearly below UConn in rigor and resources
key_quotes: Is the math department still a joke? It's still a dumpster fire
The standards are definitely higher at UConn for most STEM majors — his homework and exams were distinctly harder
From what math classes I did in undergrad, the professors weren't the greatest — had to teach myself most things due to heavy accents and incoherence

key_points: Advisors described as minimally helpful — often just send links rather than actual guidance
Registrar processes can be confusing; students navigate transfer credits largely on their own
Housing shortage communication was poor — some students found out very late
key_quotes: I emailed my advisor but she just emailed me two links... which didn't help a lot
CCSU will have a shortage for a while — I got my room but there was some type of delay
Send a succinct and informative email with specific examples to the dean, provost, and president

key_points: Located in New Britain — transit to Hartford via CT Fastrak bus is the main connection
Campus has changed noticeably over the years; parking is free with a permit
Off-campus apartments available for ~$550/month, roughly 5-minute walk to campus
key_quotes: The CT Fastrak goes directly from downtown Hartford, not far from Hartford Union Station, to campus
Looks way different compared to when I graduated in 2008 — hope people are actually landing jobs now
Off campus apartment, $550 a month per person, 10 month lease, 5 min walk from campus

key_points: Engineering job market described bluntly as 'dog shit' by one recent grad
Manufacturing engineering grads report steady employment; co-ops available in ME program
ABET accreditation means the degree is valid — but networking and employer pipelines are limited
key_quotes: Dog shit [on the engineering job market]
I did manufacturing engineering and have been steadily employed at various companies since my sophomore year
School is good for networking and resources — if the school doesn't have good networking, that's a problem

key_points: Tuition is significantly cheaper than UConn — main draw for in-state students
Free parking, now free laundry, and low-cost community college credit stacking make it affordable
Students paying ~$20k/year all-in question whether the academic quality justifies it
key_quotes: As a 24-year-old still in undergrad, the cheaper tuition [vs UConn] is a factor
I currently go to CCSU paying 20k a year for everything — are there better IT programs for around the same money?
Take community college classes cheaper by a third — the average grad takes 6 years anyway

key_points: Commuter-heavy culture — campus empties on weekends for many students
Students are practical and pragmatic, not particularly spirited about the school
Those who engage with clubs and dorm life find community; passive students feel isolated
key_quotes: I've found most of my professors actually care about teaching — if you pay attention, the professor will know you
There are different ways of meeting friends on campus — dorm life is certainly one of them
I'm in CT and there aren't a lot of options for local colleges here, at least compared to other bigger states

key_points: **Math Department:** Described as a 'dumpster fire' by multiple sources, including alumni
**Housing:** Office buildings converted to dorms due to ongoing shortage with little warning
**Specific Professors:** At least two professors (Vaz in math, unnamed in materials analysis) have serious red flags around teaching quality and workload
key_quotes: I was recommended to take math courses at Tunxis by another department chair if I really wanted to learn math
It shouldn't be impossible problem sets with 50-70 questions — that's why I left CCSU
Professor Vaz seems like he doesn't actually teach and everything is on paper — I already wanna drop the course

key_points: Spanish and Italian programs are genuinely well-regarded by students
Some professors are standout mentors — students recommend researching specific names before enrolling
Film community and chorale are active and welcoming for arts-inclined students
key_quotes: The Spanish professors are really good. The Italian programme is also pretty unique
If you have to retake materials analysis, take Prof Baumann — much better than the other option
CCSU has a relatively populous film community — looking for filmmakers who want an extra set of hands
